text:
Sentiment Analysis of Product Reviews Using Machine Learning: A Comparative Study with Negation Handling
Author : Sudesh Dahale
Department :  Information Technology, Walchand Institute of Technology, Solapur, India.
Email: sudeshdahale1008@gmail.com
Abstract—Sentiment analysis is an important task in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and is widely used in areas such as customer reviews, opinion mining, and recommendation systems. Many traditional machine learning models use a Bag-of-Words approa

ch, which often removes common stopwords during preprocessing. However, this process also removes negation words like not, no, and never. Removing these words can change the meaning of a sentence and lead to incorrect sentiment prediction. This study focuses on understanding how keeping negation-related words affects the accuracy of sentiment classification models. In this work, five machine learning models—Naive Bayes, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Support V

ector Machine (SVM)—are evaluated using the Restaurant Reviews dataset. The text data is cleaned, tokenized, stemmed, and converted into numerical features using the Bag-of-Words method. Two preprocessing methods are compared: one that removes negation words and another that keeps an extended list of negation and sentiment-changing words. Model performance is measured using accuracy and confusion matrices. The results show that all models perform better when negation words are included during pr

eprocessing. Experimental results shows that keeping negation words improves accuracy across all models from 2% to 9.5%. Random Forest gives the highest accuracy with 83.5%, followed by Logistic Regression (80.5%), SVM (78.5%), Naïve Bayes (73.5%) and KNN (68%). This confirms that preserving negation words helps models better understand sentiment and improves classification accuracy.
Keywords—Sentiment analysis, machine learning, natural language processing, negation handling, text classificatio

n, Random Forest, logistic regression, feature engineering, product reviews
I. Introduction
The rapid growth of digital platforms such as social media, online review websites, and e-commerce portals has led to an enormous increase in user-generated text data. Customers frequently express their opinions about products, services, and experiences in the form of reviews and comments. Analyzing this large volume of textual data manually is impractical, which makes automated sentiment analysis an impo

rtant research problem in Natural Language Processing (NLP).
Sentiment analysis focuses on identifying the emotional tone or opinion expressed in a piece of text [1][2]. In most real-world applications, this task is simplified into classifying text as positive or negative. Sentiment analysis is widely used in areas such as customer feedback analysis, brand monitoring, recommendation systems, and market research [2][3]. Accurate sentiment classification helps businesses understand customer satisf

action and improve decision-making.
Although deep learning techniques such as neural networks and transformers have become popular in recent years, traditional machine learning models are still widely used. These models are computationally efficient, easier to interpret, and perform well on small to medium-sized datasets. Algorithms such as Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, KNN, and SVM are commonly applied in sentiment analysis tasks.One of the major challenges in sentiment analy

sis is handling linguistic features that affect meaning, such as negation. Negation words like “not”, “never”, and “hardly” can reverse the sentiment of a sentence. For example, the sentence “The food is good” expresses a positive sentiment, while “The food is not good” expresses a negative sentiment. However, many preprocessing techniques remove negation words as part of stopword removal, which can lead to incorrect sentiment predictions.This paper focuses on studying the effect of negation han

dling in sentiment analysis. Instead of removing all stopwords, an extended set of negation and sentiment-altering words is preserved. The performance of multiple classical machine learning models is then compared to understand which model performs best on this dataset and how negation-aware preprocessing improves accuracy.
The primary objective of this paper is to analyse the effect of negation handling on traditional machine learning models. This work is the comparison between standard stopwor

d based preprocessing and negation aware preprocessing on five most common classifiers. The result shows that preserving negation and sentiment-altering words leads to consistent accuracy improvement across all models. Random Forest achieves the best results with 83.5% accuracy while Logistic Regression and SVM also shows strong results. This study highlights that even a simple linguistic enhancement during preprocessing can significantly improve the models outcome.
II. Literature Review
Negatio

n handling has long been recognized as a critical challenge in natural language processing (NLP), particularly for sentiment analysis and text classification. Early work by Polanyi and Zaenen (2006) established foundational insights into the influence of negation on semantic interpretation, highlighting how simple keyword-based approaches fail to handle complex linguistic structures where negation reverses sentiment polarity.
Pang et al. conducted one of the pioneering studies in sentiment class

ification using machine learning [1], demonstrating that traditional bag-of-words models often misclassify sentences containing negation due to the absence of context-aware parsing. Their findings underscored the need for enhanced feature engineering techniques to capture contextual dependencies introduced by negation words.
Many researchers later investigated rule-based and hybrid strategies to mitigate this problem. For instance, Wilson et al. (2005) proposed a subjectivity lexicon augmented w

ith contextual polarity modifiers, including negation, which improved sentiment extraction in product reviews and social media texts. Similarly, Zhang et al. (2012) introduced a dependency-based approach that used syntactic parsing to identify negation scopes, yielding higher accuracy over baseline models that treated negation as a separate token.
In the context of machine learning, studies have shown mixed results regarding the impact of explicit negation handling. Agarwal et al. (2011) compare

d different feature representations — including n-grams, part-of-speech tags, and negation tags — in sentiment classification. Their results confirmed that tagging negated terms significantly improves performance in support vector machines (SVMs) and logistic regression models. However, Naive Bayes models demonstrated marginal gains, suggesting that model characteristics influence how effectively negation information is utilized.
More recent advancements leverage deep learning to implicitly capt

ure negation effects. Socher et al. (2013) used recursive neural tensor networks (RNTNs) to model compositional semantics[8], achieving state-of-the-art results on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank. Their deep models inherently learn negation representations without manual feature engineering, outperforming traditional models on complex sentences containing multiple negation cues.
Despite deep learning benefits, classical algorithms remain widely used due to lower computational cost and interpreta

bility, especially in resource-constrained environments (Cambria et al., 2017). Accordingly, research into feature augmentation strategies — including negation markers, dependency features, and sentiment shifters — persists as an active area of study.
Our work builds on these foundational studies by systematically evaluating the impact of explicit negation handling across a spectrum of classical machine learning models, including Random Forest, Naive Bayes, k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN), Logistic R

egression, and Kernel SVM. Unlike prior research that focuses on individual algorithms or deep learning architectures, we offer a comparative analysis to quantify how negation processing contributes to performance improvements across diverse model families.
III. Methodology
A. Dataset and Experimental Setup
The experiments are conducted using the Restaurant Reviews dataset, which contains 1,000 customer reviews. Each review is labeled as either positive or negative. The reviews are short sentenc

es that describe customer experiences related to food quality, service, or overall satisfaction. This dataset is widely used in sentiment analysis research and is suitable for evaluating classical machine learning models.
B. Text Preprocessing Pipeline
Text preprocessing is an essential step in NLP tasks, as raw text often contains noise and irrelevant information. The preprocessing steps applied in this study include:
Removing numbers and special characters using regular expressions
Converting

all text to lowercase to maintain uniformity
Tokenizing sentences into individual words
Applying stemming using the Porter Stemmer to reduce words to their root form
Removing stopwords using the NLTK English stopword list
To analyze the effect of negation, two preprocessing configurations are used:
Without Negation:
Standard stopword removal is applied, which removes common words including negation terms.
With Negation:
An extended list of negation and sentiment-altering words is preserved. This

list includes words such as no, not, never, hardly, barely, worst, terrible, disappointed, and negative prefixes like un-, dis-, mis-. These words are retained to preserve sentiment polarity information.
Fig. 1. Sentiment analysis methodology flowchart showing preprocessing pipeline, model training, and evaluation stages.
C. Feature Extraction
After preprocessing, the text data is converted into numerical features using the Bag-of-Words (BoW) model. The CountVectorizer method is used with a max

imum of 1,500 features. This approach represents each review as a vector based on word frequency.
The dataset is then split into training and testing sets using an 80:20 ratio. The training set is used to train the models, while the testing set is used to evaluate performance.
D. Machine Learning Models
Five classical machine learning models are used in this study:
Naive Bayes: A probabilistic classifier based on Bayes’ theorem
Random Forest: An ensemble learning method using multiple decision t
rees

Logistic Regression: A linear model commonly used for binary classification
K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): A distance-based classification algorithm
Support Vector Machine (SVM): A margin-based classifier with a linear kernel
The selected models are widely used in text classification and sentiment analysis tasks due to their efficiency and proven effectiveness [4][5][6].
E. Evaluation Metrics
Model performance is evaluated using classification accuracy and confusion matrices. Accuracy measures the p

ercentage of correctly classified reviews. Confusion matrices provide detailed insight into true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives. Visual comparisons are used to highlight differences between models and preprocessing settings.
IV. Results and Discussion
A. Comparative Performance Analysis
The experimental evaluation demonstrates clear performance differences among the five machine learning models considered in this study. When negation-aware preprocessing is applie

d, all models show improved accuracy compared to their baseline versions that remove negation words. This indicates that linguistic information preserved during preprocessing plays a crucial role in sentiment classification.
Among the evaluated models, Random Forest consistently achieves the highest accuracy, reaching approximately 83.5%. This strong performance can be attributed to its ensemble nature, which combines multiple decision trees to reduce variance and improve generalization. Logisti

c Regression and Support Vector Machine (SVM) follow closely, achieving accuracies of around 80.5% and 78.5%, respectively. These models benefit from their ability to learn linear decision boundaries in high-dimensional feature spaces.
Naive Bayes and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) show comparatively lower accuracy; however, their performance remains competitive given their simplicity. Overall, the comparative analysis indicates that ensemble and linear models outperform probabilistic and distance-ba

sed approaches on the Restaurant Reviews dataset when proper preprocessing is applied.
TABLE I
COMPARATIVE ACCURACY OF SENTIMENT CLASSIFICATION MODELS
Fig. 2. Accuracy comparison across all evaluated sentiment classification models.
B. Impact of Negation Handling
Negation handling has a significant positive impact on model performance across all classifiers. When negation words are removed during standard stopword elimination, important sentiment cues are lost, often resulting in incorrect senti

ment predictions. By explicitly retaining negation and sentiment-altering words, the models are able to better capture the true meaning of reviews.
The inclusion of negation words leads to an accuracy improvement ranging from 2% to 9.5%, depending on the model. Sentences such as “not good”, “never coming back”, or “hardly worth the price” are more accurately classified when negation cues are preserved. This improvement confirms that sentiment polarity is not determined by individual words alone,

but by their interaction within a sentence.
The results highlight that even simple preprocessing changes can significantly enhance sentiment analysis performance, especially in Bag-of-Words–based models that lack contextual understanding.
C. Model-Specific Analysis
A model-wise analysis reveals distinct behavior patterns for each classifier. Random Forest shows the highest robustness to feature variation and benefits greatly from negation handling, as its tree-based structure can capture comple

x word combinations and interactions. This makes it particularly effective for datasets with mixed sentiment expressions.
Logistic Regression performs well due to its ability to assign meaningful weights to sentiment-altering words, including negation terms. When negation words are preserved, the model can better distinguish between positive and negative sentiment boundaries.
Support Vector Machine also benefits from negation handling, as it maximizes the margin between sentiment classes in a hi

gh-dimensional feature space. However, its performance is slightly lower than Logistic Regression, possibly due to sensitivity to feature sparsity.
Naive Bayes, which assumes feature independence, shows moderate improvement with negation handling. Although this assumption limits its performance, preserving negation words helps the model better estimate conditional probabilities.
KNN, being a distance-based model, benefits the least but still shows noticeable improvement, as similar sentiment pat

terns become more distinguishable in feature space.
Similar performance trends for classical machine learning models have been reported in prior sentiment analysis studies [7][10].
D. Error Analysis
Despite improved performance, some classification errors remain. Misclassifications often occur in reviews containing complex linguistic structures such as sarcasm, implicit sentiment, or mixed opinions. For example, sentences like “The food was okay, but I wouldn’t recommend it” may confuse models d

ue to the presence of both positive and negative cues.
Short reviews with limited context also contribute to errors, as Bag-of-Words models rely heavily on word frequency and lack semantic understanding. Additionally, sarcasm and irony are difficult to detect without deeper contextual or syntactic analysis.
Another source of error arises from the use of stemming, which may reduce different sentiment-altering words to the same root, potentially losing subtle sentiment differences. These observati

ons suggest that while negation handling improves performance, further enhancements such as contextual embeddings or deep learning models are required to address more complex language patterns.
V. Conclusion and Future Work
This paper presents a comparative study of classical machine learning models for sentiment analysis, with a specific focus on negation handling. The results demonstrate that explicitly retaining negation and sentiment-altering words during preprocessing significantly improves

classification accuracy for all models.
Among the evaluated models, Random Forest performs the best on the Restaurant Reviews dataset. Logistic Regression and SVM also provide competitive results, making them suitable choices for efficient sentiment analysis systems.
In the future, this work can be extended by incorporating n-gram features and TF-IDF weighting schemes to capture additional contextual information. More advanced negation handling techniques and deep learning models such as LSTM a

nd transformer-based architectures can also be explored [8][9] to further improve performance.
Vi. Acknowledgement
I would like to express sincere gratitude to the Department of Information Technology, Walchand Institute of Technology, Solapur, for providing the necessary resources and academic environment to carry out this research. I also thank Dr. P.N.Kathavate Sir for their valuable guidance and support throughout the course of this study.

[10]S. Mohammad, S. Kiritchenko, and X. Zhu, "NRC-Canada: Building the state-of-the-art in sentiment analysis of tweets," in Proc. International Workshop on Semantic Evaluation, 2013, pp. 321-327.
